Let me implement the solution. I'll create the implementation and tests in `.tmp/`.

The key design decisions:
1. Customer type needs `accountCreatedAt: Date` and `recentPurchases: Date[]` (purchases in last 90 days, or I can compute from all purchases)
2. The function replaces tier logic entirely per MJ's instruction
3. I'll keep `tier` on the Customer type as it existed before (don't break the type unnecessarily), but new logic doesn't use it

Actually, since I'm just changing the function and there's no existing file to read (it's a hypothetical codebase), I'll define the updated Customer type and function cleanly.


Now I'll write the test file and implementation with the Customer type including `accountCreatedAt` and a `purchases` array to track all purchase dates, filtering for the last 90 days within the discount calculation logic.

input.description: Check for package.json and a sample existing .tmp file for pattern reference
Check root package.json for test runner config

content.thinking:
Good, I have the pattern. Now let me write the implementation and tests.

Key design:
1. `Customer` type - keep `tier` field (it existed), add `accountCreatedAt: Date` and `purchases: Date[]`
2. `calculateDiscount(customer: Customer, now?: Date): number` - optional `now` for testability
3. Logic:
   - if accountAge < 30 days → 0.05
   - if accountAge >= 30 days AND no purchase in last 90 days → 0.03
   - if accountAge >= 30 days AND at least one purchase in last 90 days → 0

The spec explicitly says no tier-based logic, so the new function completely replaces the old one and tier won't factor into the calculation. I'll keep the `tier` field on the Customer type for backward compatibility, but the discount function won't reference it. Let me write clean, testable code.

content.text: Good — Jest + ts-jest, tests go in `.tmp/`. Now I'll write the implementation and tests.

content.content:
PASS .tmp/calculateDiscount.test.ts
  AC1: new customer discount
    ✓ returns 5% when account is 0 days old (just created) (1 ms)
    ✓ returns 5% when account is 1 day old
    ✓ returns 5% when account is 29 days old (boundary: still new)
    ✓ returns 5% even if a new customer has made purchases
    ✓ ignores tier for new customers — premium tier still gets 5%
  AC2: returning customer win-back discount
    ✓ returns 3% when account is exactly 30 days old with no purchases
    ✓ returns 3% when account is 60 days old with no purchase history
    ✓ returns 3% when last purchase was exactly 91 days ago (outside 90-day window)
    ✓ returns 3% when purchases exist but all are older than 90 days
    ✓ ignores tier for lapsed returning customers — gold tier still gets 3%
  AC3: active returning customer — no discount
    ✓ returns 0% when account is 30 days old and purchased today
    ✓ returns 0% when account is 90 days old and last purchase was 1 day ago
    ✓ returns 0% when last purchase was exactly 90 days ago (boundary: inside window) (1 ms)
    ✓ returns 0% when customer has many purchases and the latest is recent
    ✓ ignores tier for active returning customers — premium tier still gets 0%
  boundary: 30-day new/returning threshold
    ✓ 29 days old with no purchases → new customer (5%), not win-back (3%)
    ✓ 30 days old with no purchases → returning customer (3%), not new (5%)
